observations.recommendation:
We recommended, and the Management agreed to:

Require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain SLs of all real accounts and work back on the balances to obtain information/details of the following accounts:

We recommended, and Management agreed to direct:

The Inventory Committee to:
Conduct physical count semestrally.

Prepare and submit the RPCI to the accounting office and copy furnish COA.

The MGSO to:

Prepare AIR and submit to the Municipal Accountant, upon receipt of the delivery receipts and/or sales invoice for the goods delivered;

Prepare RIS, summarize the issuances, prepare SSMI, and submit the SSMI together with the RIS to the Municipal Accountant, upon issuance of goods to the end-users; and

Maintain and regularly update stock cards of inventories. SMI to the Accounting office, and other related duties deemed appropriate.

The Municipal Accountant to:

 Draw a JEV to recognize inventory and accounts payable in the books, upon receipt of the AIR and its supporting documents;

Draw a JEV to recognize the issuance of inventories to expense, upon receipt of SSMI and its supporting documents such as RIS and/or Distribution List;

Maintain and regularly update the SLCs.


Prepare JEV to record the issuances of the inventory based on the RPCI and verification on the non-existence of the inventory balance.

We recommended, and Management agreed to:

Require the Municipal Accountant to:

Investigate the cause and nature of the outstanding checks and prepare a duly supported JEV to record the adjustments; and 

Review the list of outstanding checks reflected in the monthly BRS and, if found to be stale and upon receipt of the List of Stale Checks from the Municipal Treasurer, make any necessary adjustments in the LGU’s books, review.

Require the Municipal Treasurer to regularly:

Monitor unclaimed checks and practice the sending of written notice to the payee/s at least one month before the check becomes stale.

Submit a List of Stale Checks to the Municipal Accountant for proper action in the books of accounts.

We recommended, and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to derecognize the PPE items failing the capitalization threshold by preparing the following journal entries:




We recommended, and Management agreed to instruct the engineering office to submit copies of the certificate of completion and other turnover documents to the Municipal Accountant for recording in the books of the completed PPE.

Require the Municipal Accountant to:

I. Prepare JEVs to reclassify the 100 per cent completed CIP-Infrastructure Assets amounting P23.657 million to the Water Supply Systems account in the TF books;
 
Prepare a JEV to transfer the Water Supply Systems amounting to P23.657 million from the TF to the GF by using government equity in closing and setting up of the accounts; and

Compute the related depreciation based on the completion date of the Water System as provided in the certificate of completion and prepare a JEV to recognize the accumulated depreciation for such infrastructures.


We recommended, and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:

Prepare a JEV to reclassify the completed CIP-Infrastructure Assets to the Water Supply Systems account amounting to P1.691 million and effect transfer from the TF to the GF books; and 

Locate the certificate of completion of the project to determine the basis for the computation of depreciation and prepare JEV to book up the accumulated depreciation of the infrastructure.

We recommended, and Management agreed to Instruct the engineering office to submit to the Municipal Accountant the segregated cost of the road network totaling P4,999 million into the following, if applicable:
1.	Road lot
2.	Road pavement 
3.	Drainage and slope protection structures
4.	Other miscellaneous structure

Require the Municipal Accountant to:

Prepare the following JEV.





Locate the certificate of completion of the project to determine the basis for the computation of depreciation and prepare JEV to book up the accumulated depreciation of the infrastructure.

We recommended, and Management agreed to instruct:

The Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant a certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year every beginning of the year;
 
The Municipal Accountant to set-up the RPT/SET Receivables together with its corresponding deferrals at the beginning of the year using the certified list to be provided by the Municipal Treasurer; and

The Municipal Accountant to prepare JEV to correct the RPT Receivables and Deferrals, and SET Receivables and Deferrals, in the GF and TF books, respectively.


We recommended, and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:

Accurately derecognize the RPT/SET Receivables at gross amount upon collection of RPT/SET; and

Effect corrections by an adjustment to RPT/SET Receivables together with its corresponding deferrals.



We recommended, and Management agreed to require The Municipal Treasurer to regularly update the RPTAR and provide complete data therein, and provide the Municipal Accountant with tax delinquency data pertaining to RPT and SET

b. Require the Municipal Accountant to:

Prepare an aging report of delinquent RPT and SET to determine impaired RPT/SET Receivables; and 

Provide an allowance for impairment if warranted, to present an accurate account balance for these receivables in the FSs of the Municipality by debiting Deferred RPT/SET and crediting Allowance for Impairment – RPT/SET Receivable.

We recommended, and Management agreed to direct:

The Inventory Committee to:

Conduct the annual physical inventory of PPEs under the name of the LGU and relieve each member of their regular functions to devote themselves in completing the same;
 
Prepare the PIP, and submit the same to the Municipal Mayor to ensure that the conduct of inventory-taking is properly and adequately planned pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006;

Submit a copy of the approved PIP to COA at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities;

Use pre-numbered property stickers bearing the vital information on the PPE items, such as

a)	Description of the Property
b)	Model Number
c)	Serial Number
d)	Acquisition Date / Cost
e)	Person Accountable
f)	Space for the Validation / Signature of the Inventory Committee in the conduct of physical inventory and attach the same to the prominent and secured portion of the inventoried PPE for proper and easy identification;
 
Prepare a consolidated RPCPPE which will be the basis for the reconciliation of PPE items with the balances appearing in the accounting and property records, and consequently, investigate discrepancies thereof; and
 
Strictly implement COA Circular No. 2020-006 in the conduct of inventory of PPE.

OIC-Municipal GSO to:
 
Use pre-numbered property tags or control stickers to all PPE containing the information required under Section 5.7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006 and attach the same to the prominent and secured portion of the inventoried PPE for proper and easy identification;
 
Ensure that all issuances of PPE items are covered with Property Acknowledgement Receipts (PARs) as prescribed under Section 5.6 of COA Circular No. 2020-006; and

Renew/Update issued PARs every three (3) years after issuance or whenever there is a change in custodianship to establish proper accountability.

Municipal GSO, Municipal Accountant and Inventory Committee to coordinate and complete the physical inventory-taking and prepare the RPCPPE and consequently conduct the reconciliation of the results thereof; identify and address the nature and causes of variances; and hold responsible/accountable the designated officers/custodians; and

Municipal Accountant to record adjustments after completion of proper reconciliation to establish the correct balances of PPE and other affected accounts, with the presence of valid supporting documents.

We restated our recommendation, and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Office to conduct monthly reconciliation of their records to facilitate the preparation of the Reconciliation Between Actual and Budgeted Amounts of the SCBAA for disclosure in the Notes to FSs.

We reiterated our recommendation. and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to disclose the sources and utilization of DRRMF in the notes to the FSs, in prescribed format under Annex D, as provided by paragraph 5.1.16 COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended, and Management agreed to:

Allocate budget for the titling process of the land of the Municipality; and
 
Direct the Municipal Engineer to facilitate the survey of lands and submit the survey plans to the Municipal Treasurer/MGSO and proceed with the titling of such lands.

We reiterated our recommendation, and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:

Prepare monthly bank reconciliations of the bank accounts under   TF and SEF and submit the same to the Audit Team, in accordance with Section 74 of PD 1445 and items 3.2 and 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011;
 
Prepare the necessary journal entries to reflect the corrections in the books as a result of the bank reconciliation, including the noted unrecorded checks; and

Establish, maintain and/or update the SLs of the 11 cash in bank and three CIT accounts as required by Sections 111 and 114 (2) of PD 1445 and Section 310 of RA 7160.

We reiterated our recommendation, and Management agreed to require:

The Municipal Treasurer to reconcile all its Cashbooks balances with the SLs maintained by the Municipal Accountant at the end of each month or as the need arises.
 
The Municipal Accountant to affix signature below the words “Balance is the same with SL,” or any statement which would mean that monthly balance reflected in the Cashbooks, is the same as – or is reconciled – with what appeared in the SLs maintained by the accounting office.

We recommended, and Management agreed to require:

The Municipal Budget Officer and all the concerned end-users to prepare PPMPs for their department’s expected procurement needs in the succeeding budget year and submit the PPMPs to the Municipal Budget Officer for budget consideration; and
 
The Municipal Budget Office shall submit to the BAC thru the BAC Secretariat all PPMPs received from the end-users for consolidation into APP in accordance with the provisions of Section 7.3.2. and 7.3.4. of Rule II of the RIRR of RA 9184.



We recommended, and Management agreed to require:

The end-users to completely fill-out the information in the PR such as the date, fund source, purpose, and whether the PR has available allotment from Municipal Budget Office to comply with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Section 116, Chapter 7, of the MNGAS for LGUs; and
 
The Municipal Treasury’s Office, the Municipal Budget’s Office, the Municipal Mayor’s Office, the MGSO, and the BAC Secretariat Office, to review the PRs before affixing their signatures and return the document to the end-user or to the concerned office should the pre-requisite field has not been properly filled out.

We recommended, and Management agreed to require: 

the BAC that the recommendation on the use of the Alternative Mode of Procurement be done through a resolution bearing the signatures of all the members of the BAC who are present and who participated in the deliberation;
 
that the HoPE’s approval of the Resolution must also appear in that single document, to comply with Section 48.3, Rule XVI of the RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended, and Management agreed to require the BAC to observe the proper mode from among the Alternative Mode of Procurement as provided by Sections V(C)(1) and V(C)(8)(a) of Annex “H” of the RIRR of RA 9184 in the procurement of goods.

We recommended, and Management agreed to require the BAC, through the Secretariat, to make the necessary postings, for information purposes, of the notice of awards, the purchase orders, and the RFQs, on the PhilGEPS website, and at any conspicuous places reserved for this purpose in the premises of the procuring entity for period of at least three (3) calendar days for SVP with ABC above Fifty Thousand Pesos (₱50,000.00) as required by Section 54.3, Rule XV and Section IV(E)(3) Annex “H” of the RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended, and Management agreed to require the BAC, through the Secretariat, to include the supporting documents of the payments for the procurement, through Shopping, the Mayor’s/ Business Permit and PhilGEPS Registration Number as required by Section II, Annex “H” of the RIRR of RA 9184.

We recommended, and Management agreed to require the BAC, through the Secretariat, to indicate in the PO the important details such as the Mode of Procurement, and the Date and the Terms of Delivery to comply with COA Circular No. 96-010.

We restated our recommendation, and Management agreed to:

 Direct the Municipal Engineer to fast track the implementation of the Unimplemented or delayed projects/programs under the CY 2023 20 per cent DF current and continuing appropriations.
 
Ensure that PPAs charged to 20 per cent DF should be well-planned and procurement – and – implementation ready, moving forward.

Moving forward, we recommended, and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare a DV from the GF to transfer the unexpended and unobligated LDRRMF balance at year-end and the Municipal Treasurer to draw a check to effect the transfer at year-end.

Moving forward, we recommended, and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to properly monitor the available budget and allotment for the LDRRMF to prevent the charging of activities and disbursement thereof beyond the appropriated funds.

We recommended, and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to:

Require business owners to submit a sworn statement of gross sales/receipts, along with Audited FSs and/or ITR, if applicable, for the renewal of businesses, and a sworn statement of capital expenditures for newly started businesses, and assessment of business taxes shall be based thereon; and
 
Conduct examination on books of accounts of business taxpayers in cases of under declaration of gross receipts to establish effective enforcement and collection of taxes, fees and charges as provided in the Ordinance.

We recommended, and management agreed that the Solid Waste Management Board direct the responsible person to follow-up the application for ECC on the site or area used by the Municipality for solid waste disposal, to comply with Section 48(14), Chapter VI of RA 9003.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Require the official drivers to timely and regularly prepare and submit duly accomplished and serially-numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets for each trip, Pre-numbered gas Slip, and the corresponding copy of Delivery Receipt for control purposes. This document will show the details of the actual places visited, the actual distance traveled, and the corresponding quantity of fuel consumed. A summary record of travel is prepared for every vehicle with complete supporting documents;
 
Require the OIC-GSO to timely and regularly prepare the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption Report for monitoring and controlling purposes, and to submit these reports to the Auditor for verification and evaluation of the reasonableness of fuel consumption in a specific month; and

Assign an official to closely monitor and control fuel consumption, and enjoin compliance with the above -stated regulations in order to properly account for the utilization of gasoline, oil and lubricants.

We reiterated our recommendation that management require the GAD focal person to strictly monitor the implementation of the GAD Plan and Budget PPAs to be able to accomplish the utilization of at least 5% of the municipality’s annual budget to comply with PCW-NEDA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2012-01.

The management to submit immediately their explanations and/or justification on the noted discrepancies supported with the necessary attachments in order for the team to make a decision on the additional and necessary courses of action to be taken regarding the computed excess cost of the aforementioned project versus COA estimate.

Management provide the audit team with the updated status of actions taken by the Municipality for the full settlement of the disallowance, as a compliance with the June 14, 2021 letter of the COA LSS Prosecution and Litigation Office; 


Management review the funding status of unpaid employee benefits, such as terminal leave pay, of the responsible personnel under COE dated June 29, 2017, and if there’s none, prioritize appropriation of such in the ensuing year’s budget, so that such funds be immediately applied to the partial, if not total, settlement of the disallowance.

The Municipal Mayor issue an Executive or Administrative Order creating the Monitoring and Evaluation Team in consonance with the provisions of item 5 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 in order to implement the GAD PPAs efficiently and effectively.

The Municipal Mayor direct the responsible committee to evaluate the current composition of the MSWMB and look for possible members that could qualify to complete the line up and ensure that the Board shall perform its tasks and functions as mandated by RA 9003
The municipality’s development council headed by the Local Chief Executive shall ensure the optimum implementation of the programs/projects/activities funded out of the Municipality’s 20% DF.

The Municipal Mayor through the BAC Secretariat to post all the required information in the GPPB Online Portal for the negotiated procurement under emergency cases as enumerated in Table 6 and post it in conspicuous places within the premises of the Municipality with easy access of the public to comply with the requirements of Sections 3.6 and 9 of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020.

Likewise, the OIC, Municipal Accountant to review the supporting documents before processing of payment to ensure completeness of supporting documents.

the BAC Secretariat to ensure that OSS shall be executed by all bidders/suppliers contracted for negotiated procurement under emergency cases either 1) upon submission of offers, 2) during evaluation of offers, 3) before issuance of NOA, or 4) prior to payment and original must be attached.

Likewise, the OIC, Municipal Accountant to review the supporting documents before processing of payment to ensure completeness of supporting documents.

We recommended, and Management through the Municipal Accountant, agreed to determine the composition of the undetermined items totaling P36.226 million for GF upon completion on the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE.

We recommended, and Management through the Municipal Accountant agreed to coordinate with the Municipal General Service Officer (MGSO) to trace the records of PPE items, if there is any, and prepare the required supporting schedules on six (6) PPE accounts totaling P97,374.23 under the SEF and 11 PPE accounts totaling P151.353 million under the TF.

We recommended, and Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Engineer to submit to the Municipal Accountant the Certificate of Project Completion as basis in reclassifying or transferring of CIP to its appropriate asset account; and
Upon receipt of the Certificate of Project Completion from the Municipal Engineer, the Municipal Accountant to prepare Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the reclassification of completed CIP items and recognition of related accumulated depreciation;
We also recommended, and both the Municipal Engineering Office and the Municipal Accounting Office agreed to regularly coordinate and reconcile their records on the status of all on-going and completed projects at least quarterly.

We recommended, and Management, through and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare the following adjusting entry to properly set-up the depreciation on each item in the Road Network account:
We also recommended, and Management agreed that the Municipal Engineer provide the Municipal Accountant with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects, for proper recording in the books of accounts.

We recommended, and Management agreed that the Municipal Assessor and the MPDC coordinate with the Municipal Engineer in identifying the road lots under the LGU, and determine its cost, (if the costs is not available), its market value; and
Thereafter, submit the report to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the proper recording of these assets in the books of accounts.

We recommended, and Management agreed to the following:
The IC to conduct physical count of Local Road Network, starting with the road network system recorded in the books. In the conduct of physical count, determine each component of the local roads, its costs or fair market value (if without identified costs), and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network;
The Municipal Engineer to provide the MGSO with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects;
The MGSO at the end or every accounting period to render a Report on Local Road Network using the pro-forma schedule in Annex A and maintain a local road inventory, Road Map and complete Road Network Property Card (Annex E) for all roads and its components, upon receipt of the list from the Municipal Engineer;
The Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare and maintain the LRNLC (Annex D) separately from each of the road component namely: a) road lot; b) road pavement; c) drainage and slope protection structures; and d) other miscellaneous structures, upon receipt of the list from the Municipal Engineer; and
Prepare the necessary disclosure in the Notes to FS on Local Road Networks as prescribe in Annex B COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.

We recommended, and Management agreed to the following:
The MGSO:
To secure the distribution lists on inventories held for distribution that were issued during the pandemic from the end users and prepare SSMI to be submitted to the accounting unit;
For future transactions, upon issuance of goods to the end-users, to summarize the issuances and prepare SSMI on the basis of the approved RIS, and submit same to the Municipal Accountant; and
To Maintain and regularly update stock cards of inventories.
The Municipal Accountant:
To prepare JEV to adjust overstatement of Inventory and understatement of Prior Year’s Adjustment accounts by P19.584 million upon receipt of SSMI supported by Distribution List and/or RIS;
For future transactions, upon receipt of SSMI and its supporting documents such as RIS and/or Distribution List, draw a JEV to recognize the issuance of inventories to expense; and
Maintain and regularly update the supplies ledger cards.
The IC to conduct physical count of inventories and attest to the balances of the reported inventories in the FS.

We recommended, and Management agreed to, the following:
The HRMO, during budget preparations of the Annual Budget, include not only the retirable/co-terminous employees and elected officials during the Annual Budget period but also those employees/officials who will retire and whose term of service will end in the ensuing year in the computation of the terminal leave benefits;
The Local Finance Committee (LFC) prioritize the allocation of leave benefits computed by the HRMO in the Annual Budget; and
The Municipal Accountant:
Recognize in the books of accounts the unrecorded leave benefits payable and subsequently, the leave benefits which are expected to be settled within 12 months after the end of the reporting period supported by certification from the Municipal Budget Officer and Treasurer on the availability of appropriation and funds. The proposed adjusting entries are as follows:
Prior Period Adjustments			P 5,225,515.84		Leave Benefits Payable			P5,225,515.84		To recognize prior years’ accumulated earned leave credits
as of December 31, 2024.
Suggested accounting entries upon payment:
Leave Benefits Payable
Terminal Leave Benefits*
Prior Period Adjustments**
Cash in Bank
To record payment of accumulated earned leave credits
*Leave credits earned during CY 2025
** PYs’ leave benefits due to increase of salary in CY 2025
Ensure compliance with IPSAS 1 and IPSAS 20 by providing the required disclosures in the Notes to FS, specifically on the aggregate renumeration of key management and those who are already retired for the year and those that are retiring in the next reporting period, as well as the Municipality’s adoption of IPSAS 39 in accordance with COA Circular No. 2024-009; and
Provide disclosure in the notes to FS on the nature of the leave benefits payable and its expected payment for at least the following year.

We recommended, and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to rectify the affected accounts and correct the errors from the previously recorded entries.
Properly examine and verify all reconciling items before recording it in the books of accounts.

We recommended, and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant:
Conduct a comprehensive review of the CIB-LCCA, and, upon completion, make necessary adjustments ensuring that all entries are accurately supported by schedules and relevant documents.
Prepare monthly bank reconciliations of the bank accounts under GF, TF and SEF and submit the same to the Audit Team, in accordance with Section 74 of PD 1445 and items 3.2 and 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011.

We recommended, and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant establish and maintain the SLs of all 14 cash in bank accounts as required by Sections 111 and 114 (2) of PD 1445 and Section 310 of RA 7160.

We recommended, and Management agreed to the following:
The Office of the Municipal Accountant:
Trace the DVs of the stale checks to gather the nature of the transaction and name of payees, and prepare JEV to record the adjustments, duly supported by a schedule of stale checks; and
Prepare the BRS monthly, monitor and inform the Municipal Treasurer of the movement of outstanding checks.
The MTO, upon receipt of the list of outstanding checks from the Municipal Accountant, notify the payees of their checks of its status, and when necessary for cancellation and replacement of their checks.

We recommended, and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
Draw a JEV to effect necessary adjustments in the books of accounts in CY 2025 as follows:
Maintain a schedule of SL for Accounts Payable to properly track payables and ensure that payments are applied to previously recorded obligations; and
Perform regular reconciliation between the Subsidiary Schedule and the general ledger to verify the accuracy of recorded transactions.

We recommended, and Managemen,t through the LFC agreed to:
Discontinue the practice of charging prior year’s expenses to current year appropriations;
Establish a strict year-end cut-off for claims by setting an earlier internal deadline for its submission to the Municipal Budget Office and Accountant, and enhance monitoring procedures to encourage coordination among departments; and
Inform the Local Sangguniang Bayan (SB) to stop authorizing the payment of unobligated expenditures from previous years using the current year appropriations following strictly Section 119 of PD 1445 and Section 350 of RA 7160.

We recommended, and the Municipal Accountant agreed to trace the transactions that caused the abnormal balance of these accounts and to reconcile these liability accounts namely; Due to BIR, Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG, Due to PhilHealth, and Due to NGAs accounts, respectively.

We recommended, and Management agreed that:
The Municipal Treasurer to fast track the completion of the RPTAR and Certified List of Taxpayers, and provide it to the Municipal Accountant as a proper basis for RPT and SET receivables; and
Upon receipt of the RPTAR and Certified list of Taxpayers the Municipal Accountant shall review and reconcile the book balance against former to come up with accurate RPT/SET Receivable and its Deferred.

We recommended, and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare a DV from GF to transfer the remaining unexpended and unobligated LDRRMF balance at year-end to the Trust Liabilities-DRRMF under the TF books and the Municipal Treasurer to draw a check to effect the transfer.

We reiterate our recommendation, and the Municipal Accountant agreed to provide disclosures in the Notes to FS on the following:
The sources and utilization of DRRMF in the notes to the FS, in prescribed format under Annex D, as provided by paragraph 5.1.16 COA Circular No. 2012-002.
The Reconciliation of the SCBAA and Statement of Financial Performance as per Section 3.20 of Annex G, COA Circular 2016-004.
The implementation of the above-mentioned resolution along with EO 138, their details, financial, and non-financial effects to the LGU, and revise the account name of the account “Share from Internal Revenue Allotment” to “Share from National Tax Allotment” in compliance with the above-mentioned resolution and EO.
Also, we recommended, and the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer agreed to conduct quarterly reconciliations of their records to facilitate easier preparation of reconciliation of SCBAA and SFPer, as this approach ensures that records are matched gradually rather than all at once for the entire year.

We recommended, and management agreed that:
The IC conduct physical count of PPE and identify PPEs that are unserviceable.
The LCE reconstitute the Appraisal and Disposal Committee.
The GSO prepare IIRUP and submit the same to the disposal committee for the immediate appraisal and disposal of all unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in the items of RMDGP of COA-DBM JC No. 2024-1.

We recommended, and Management agreed to adhere to the provisions of COA Circular 2020-006 on the one-time cleansing of the PPE account of the Municipality, specifically, the following:
The LCE to:
Reconstitute the member of the IC; and
Temporarily relieve the members of the IC to all their regular duties to prioritize the completion of physical inventory count in accordance with the procedures in COA Circular 2020-006.
The IC to:
Prepare the PIP, and submit the same to the Municipal Mayor for approval to ensure that the conduct of inventory-taking is properly and adequately planned pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006;
Submit a copy of the approved PIP to COA at least ten (10) calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities; and
Conduct a complete physical count of PPE based on the guidelines and procedures provided in COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended, and Management agreed that:
The Municipal Mayor:
Issue a memorandum order enjoining all officers and employees with unliquidated cash advances to immediately liquidate the same with a provision instructing the Municipal Treasurer to hold their claims/salaries unless their CAs are fully liquidated;
Issue demand letter those employees who transferred from, or are no longer connected with the municipality, and request assistance to the agency they are currently connected with for the collection of the accountable officers’ outstanding cash advances; and
Through the Municipal Accountant, to submit to COA a request for write-off of cash advances of deceased AOs duly supported by death certificate pursuant to COA Circular 2016-005.
For past due Cash advances aging more than 10 years, instruct the accounting office to review the probability of collection for AOs with inactive AOs status and if remote, make a formal request to COA for write-off of dormant unliquidated cash advances which remained non-moving for ten (10) years or more pursuant to COA Circular 2016-005.
The Municipal Accountant:
Regularly monitor the movement of the cash advances of concerned AOs and ensure that prior to grant of additional cash advances to AOs with unliquidated cash advances, a certification in the face of the DV is made that the AO has not unliquidated CA;
Trace the balances of cash advances and maintain SLs.
We recommended, and Management agreed to stop the granting of any additional allowances, incentives, or other financial benefits to officials and employees unless expressly authorized by law.

We recommended, and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the following adjusting entries on the actual transfer cash of P3.049 million unspent CY 2023 QRF, both in the GF and TF books, to wit:

We recommended, and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to make the following entries to correct the overstatement of the Due from Officers and Employees by P2.412 million and understatement of Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account by the same amount:

We recommended, and Management agreed that the GFPS thru the LCE endorse to the Committee on Family, Women, Children and Elderly of the SB, the passing an ordinance localizing RA No. 11313, which if passed, shall be posted in conspicuous places. Further, include in the ordinance the following: a) measures to prevent gender-based sexual harassment in educational institutions, b) imposition of fines/penalty for violation thereof, and c) creation of sexual harassment hotlines.

Instruct the accounting personnel assigned to journalize simultaneously the financial transactions affecting the reciprocal accounts Due from and Due to Other Funds in the different funds affected, moving forward; and
The Municipal Government thru the Municipal Mayor to Reconstitute the composition of the existing GFPS Execom to comply with item 4 of PCW-DILG-DBM and NEDA JMC No. 2013-01;
Implemented

1. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the accounting office to promptly investigate the cause of the P411,108 discrepancy between the amounts reflected in the Bank Reconciliation Statements and the financial statements under the general fund and submit to the audit team the JEV of the adjustments resulting therefrom.

2. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and provide monthly bank reconciliation statements for the seven TF and one SEF bank accounts to the Audit Team, in accordance with Section 74 of PD 1445 and items 3.2 and 3.4 of COA Circular No. 96-011.

3. We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to establish and maintain SLs of the noted 7 Trust Fund bank accounts as required by Sections 111 and 114 (2) of PD 1445 and Section 310 of RA 7160.

We reiterated our recommendation for the Municipal Mayor to:

4. Require the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile all its Cb balances with the SLs maintained by the Municipal Accountant at the end of each month or as the need arises.

5. Require the Municipal Accountant to affix her signature below the words “Balance is the same with Subsidiary Ledger,” or any statement which would mean that monthly balance reflected in the Cb, is the same as – or is reconciled – with what appeared in the SLs maintained by her.

6. We recommended for the Local Chief Executive to craft a policy requiring strict compliance with Section 4(a) of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I and COA Circular No. 97-002 by issuing a memorandum order every December of the year providing for the cut-off, among others, on the submission of claims and liquidation of cash advances with the accounting office, moving forward.

Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to instruct:
The Municipal Treasurer to prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant a certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year every beginning of the year; and
The Municipal Accountant to set-up the RPT/SET Receivables together with its corresponding deferrals at the beginning of the year using the certified list to be provided by the Municipal Treasurer; and
The Municipal Accountant to prepare JEV to correct the RPT Receivables and Deferrals, and SET Receivables and Deferrals, in the GF and TF books, respectively.
Improper reversal entries to derecognize RPT/SET Receivables and its corresponding Deferred RPT/SET upon collection thus, RPT Receivables and Deferred RPT, and SET Receivables and Deferred RPT, were overstated and understated by P177,234, and P12,260, respectively.
Section 20 of the NGAS Manual providing the basis of recording RPT/SET states that, "Upon collection of Real Property Taxes from taxpayers, the account Deferred Real Property Tax Income/Deferred Special Education Tax Income shall be debited while the Real Property Tax Income due to the municipality is recognized/credited. The share of the Province and Barangay shall also be credited to Due to LGUs".
Furthermore, Section 36 of the same manual also provides the following pro-forma accounting entries to record the receipt and distribution of collections:
We reviewed the entries made by the Municipal Accountant upon collection of RPT and SET and noted the following:
Upon receipt of collection of RPT/SET, the following compound entries were made:
Simultaneously, the Accounting records the reversal of both the RPT/SET receivable and the Deferred RPT/SET with the following entries:
During the year, the RPT Receivable and SET Receivable together with its corresponding deferrals derecognized totaled to P109,360.00 and P295,125.00, respectively. This represents the share of the Municipality only in the collection of RPT and SET, based on the amount collected, net of discounts, if any.
Notably, in the case of advance payments, the discount shall be recorded as a debit to "Discount on RPT/SET," which is a deduction from the amount of cash received. Similarly, upon distribution of the collection, a portion of the said discount shall be apportioned to the respective LGUs in accordance with the sharing prescribed for real property tax as per Section 22 of the NGAS Manual.
As evident from observations (a) and (b), the Accounting Department incorrectly recognizes both income and liability for the share of both the province and the barangay upon collection. This approach is inconsistent with Section 36 of the NGAS (New Government Accounting System) Manual. According to this section, income and liability for the share of both the province and the barangay should only be recognized upon reversal or derecognition of Deferred RPT/SET.
Furthermore, the Accounting Department should have recognized or reversed first the RPT/SET Receivable upon recognition of the amount collected gross of any discount.
Our re-computation of the 'should be' reversal of RPT/SET Receivables, together with its corresponding deferrals based on the total gross collected amount of RPT and SET revealed that these accounts were overstated due to the understated reversal entries made upon receipt of collections, as illustrated below:
The erroneous reversal of RPT/SET receivables and their deferral upon collection could ultimately lead to an overstatement of these accounts at year-end, thereby compromising their accuracy and reliability.
Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:
Accurately derecognize the RPT/SET Receivables at gross amount upon collection of RPT/SET; and
Effect corrections by an adjustment to RPT/SET Receivables together with its corresponding deferrals.
No allowance for impairment was recognized for the prescribed RPT Receivables and SET Receivables totaling by at least P145,572, each thus, overstating the said accounts by the same amount. Moreover, not all RPTAR maintained by Municipal Treasurer were updated.
The IPSAS 29 states that:
Par. 67. An entity shall apply the impairment in paragraphs 73-79 to financial assets that are measured at amortized cost in accordance with paragraph 40 and to financial assets that are measured at fair value through net assets/equity in accordance with paragraph 41.
Par. 73. An entity shall recognize a loss allowance for expected credit losses on a financial asset that is measured in accordance with paragraphs 40 or 41, a lease receivable, or a loan commitment and a financial guarantee contract xxx
Furthermore, COA Circular No. 2015-001 provides the account Allowance for Impairment – Real Property Tax Receivable described as:
This account is credited when there is objective evidence that the real property tax is impaired. Debit this account for collection of receivables, transfer or reversal of impairment.
Likewise, Section 270 of RA 7160 also provides that, “The basic real property tax and any other tax levied under this Title shall be collected within five (5) years from the date they become due. No action for the collection of the tax, whether administrative or judicial, shall be instituted after the expiration of such period. xxx”
We reviewed the unpaid taxes of various taxpayers and compared them to the RPTAR as maintained by the Treasury Department. We noted that:
Some of the RPTAR were not updated and some were blank or have no entries.
Inquiry with the Municipal Treasurer disclosed that their office has already sent out demands and notices to various delinquent taxpayers. Received copies of which are yet to be forwarded to the audit team for verification.
Based on the updated RPTARs only, the audit team prepared an aging schedule to 664 RPUs and it was disclosed that:
As noted in the aging, there were RPT and SET which remained unpaid by RPU owners were only as far back as 2005.
Our review of the FSs for CY 2023 revealed that despite the lapse of the 5-year period to collect these delinquent tax dues, there was no allowance for impairment provided by the management. Furthermore, no aging was prepared, nor was there any disclosure in the notes to the FS regarding the RPT and SET receivables. When we made an inquiry with the Municipal Accountant, it was disclosed that they are currently reconciling the records maintained by the Assessor and the Treasurer and converting them into electronic format since they were prepared manually.
Although the Municipality has initiated demands for payment of delinquent RPT and SET, the collection in CY 2023 has declined as compared to the previous year (CY 2022). The comparison of this collection based on the comparative income as recognized in the said years is shown below:
As can be gleaned from the preceding table, the total collections in the current year had decreased by P41,573. This collection performance by the management is indicative that despite the demands/notices sent out in the current year, the total annual collection was still declining.
During our interview with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office personnel, it was also disclosed that no indorsements were made to the province for the levy of properties with unpaid RPT and SET despite issuance of demands or notices of tax delinquencies.
It is evident that while Management has taken steps to initiate collection efforts, the likelihood of successful collection remains uncertain based on the preceding circumstances. Therefore, it is prudent for the Municipality to explore the feasibility of establishing an allowance for impairment for the unpaid RPT and SET amounts beyond the prescription period, as stipulated in Section 270 of RA 7160. By doing so, the Municipality can accurately reflect the potential risks associated with these outstanding taxes and ensure a more realistic representation of its FS.
Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to require:
The Municipal Treasurer to regularly update the RPTAR and provide complete data therein, and provide the Municipal Accountant with tax delinquency data pertaining to RPT and SET;
The Municipal Accountant to:
Prepare an aging report of delinquent RPT and SET to determine impaired RPT/SET Receivables; and
Provide an allowance for impairment if warranted, to present an accurate account balance for these receivables in the FSs of the Municipality by debiting Deferred RPT/SET and crediting Allowance for Impairment – RPT/SET Receivable.

Important details on the Purchase Requests (PR) such as the date, fund source, purpose, and whether the PR has available allotment from Municipal Budget Office are not indicated
The PD 1445, Section 4(6) provides that, “[c]laims against government funds shall be supported with complete documentation.”
“Completeness of the documentation” presupposes that all the fields therein are duly filled out.
Sec. 116, Chapter 7, The MNGAS for LGUs states:
Requisition Procedures. – (a) Requirement of Requisition - Any order for supplies shall be filled by the provincial GSO, the city GSO, or the municipal treasurer, as the case maybe, for any office or department of the LGU concerned only upon written requisition.
(b) Forms to be used - Requisitions shall be accomplished using the following forms:
(1)	Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) - for supplies carried in stock; and
(2)	Purchase Request (PR) - for supplies not carried in stock.
(d) Certification on Allotment and Obligation Slip (ALOBS) - Every PR must be accompanied by an ALOBS showing the certification of the local budget officer and the local accountant, that an appropriation therefore exists; and that the estimated amount of such expenditure has been obligated. The Local Treasurer shall certify as to cash availability in the purchase request.
Review of the PRs on the procurements disclosed that the end-users did not indicate the important details such as the date, the fund source, the purpose, and whether the PR has available allotment from Municipal Budget Officer.
The document has not been returned to the end-user or to the office responsible for correction nor completing the information in the PRs before PRs are certified by the Municipal Treasurer, approved by the Municipal Mayor or upon receipt of the PRs by the Municipal GSO and BAC. Thus, presupposes lack of review of the responsible officials before affixing their signatures.
Recommendations
We recommended, and Management agreed to require:
The end-users to completely fill-out the information in the PR such as the date, fund source, purpose, and whether the PR has available allotment from Municipal Budget Office to comply with Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Section 116, Chapter 7, of the MNGAS for LGUs; and
The Municipal Treasury’s Office, the Municipal Budget’s Office, the Municipal Mayor’s Office, the MGSO, and the BAC Secretariat Office, to review the PRs before affixing their signatures and return the document to the end-user or to the concerned office should the pre-requisite field has not been properly filled out.
The Approval for the change of mode of procurement other than public bidding is not made thru BAC Resolution
Section 48.3, Rule XVI of the RIRR of RA 9184 states:
… If the original method of procurement recommended in the APP was competitive bidding but cannot be ultimately pursued, the BAC, through a resolution, shall justify and recommend the change in the method of procurement to be approved by the HoPE.
Currently, as regards procurement of various supplies and materials used for the repair, maintenance, and improvement services of various vehicles, heavy equipment, the municipal plaza, the water supply system, and the gymnasium, which were not included in the APPP for the year, when the BAC identifies that the PR is eligible for the alternative method of procurement the BAC Chairperson sends a correspondence to the HoPE recommending that the procurement be done through the alternative means. The HoPE then sends a separate letter approving the recommendation.
The requirement, however, is for the BAC, not just the BAC Chairperson, communicates through a RESOLUTION, not just through a letter signed by the Chairperson himself, recommending that the procurement be done on alternative mode and the HoPE, in the same document, approves the recommendation.
It must be emphasized that all the signatures of the BAC who are present and who participated in the deliberation, recommending the approval for the use of the alternative means of procurement and the approval of the HoPE must appear in one, single document bearing the resolution thereof.
Recommendation
We recommended, and Management agreed to require: (a) the BAC that the recommendation on the use of the Alternative Mode of Procurement be done through a resolution bearing the signatures of all the members of the BAC who are present and who participated in the deliberation; (b) and, that the HoPE’s approval of the Resolution must also appear in that single document, to comply with Section 48.3, Rule XVI of the RIRR of RA 9184.
The BAC, and as approved by the HoPE, erroneously resorted to Shopping instead of Small Value Procurement (SVP)
Relevant provisions of Annex “H” of the RIRR of RA 9184 state the following:
Section V(C)(1) defined Shopping as a method of procurement of goods whereby the Procuring Entity simply requests for the submission of price quotations for readily available off-the-shelf goods or ordinary/regular equipment to be procured directly from suppliers of known qualifications. This method of procurement shall be employed in any of the following cases:
b) Procurement of ordinary or regular office supplies and equipment
The phrase “ordinary or regular office supplies” shall be understood to include those supplies, commodities, or materials which are necessary in the transaction of its official businesses, and consumed in the day-to-day office operations.
However, office supplies shall not include services such as repair and maintenance of equipment and furniture, as well as trucking, hauling, janitorial, security, and related or analogous services.
Section V(C)(8)(a) defined Small Value Procurement (SVP) as the [p]rocurement of (a) goods not covered by Shopping under Section 52 of the IRR of RA 9184…
Evaluation of the procurements disclosed that these do not qualify for Shopping as these falls outside the definition of “ordinary or regular office supplies”. On the contrary, these were used for the repair and maintenance services of the said vehicles, heavy equipment, municipal plaza, water supply system, and gymnasium, which should have fall under SVP, save for exceptions on threshold amount.
There is a need to differentiate the Shopping from the SVP because the latter requires Mayor’s/Business Permit, Professional License/ Curriculum Vitae (Consulting Services), PhilGEPS Reg. Number, Philippine Contractors Accreditation Board (PCAB) License (Infra.), and Income/ Business Tax Return and Omnibus Sworn Statement (for Approved Budget for the Contract (ABCs) above P50,000.00) to be complied with while the former only requires the Mayor’s/Business Permit and the PhilGEPS Reg. Number. Thus, the erroneous resort to Shopping on procurement of services, instead of the SVP, means that the supporting documents for the eligibility of suppliers prior to award of contract were inadequate.
Recommendation
We recommended, and Management agreed to require the BAC to observe the proper mode from among the Alternative Mode of Procurement as provided by Sections V(C)(1) and V(C)(8)(a) of Annex “H” of the RIRR of RA 9184 in the procurement of goods.
The procurement was not posted on the PhilGEPS website
Section 54.3, Rule XV, of the RIRR of RA 9184 states that:
In all instances of alternative methods of procurement, the BAC, through the Secretariat, shall post, for information purposes, the notice of award, contract or purchase order, including notice to proceed if necessary, in the PhilGEPS website, the website of the Procuring Entity concerned, if available, and at any conspicuous place reserved for this purpose in the premises of the Procuring Entity, except for contracts with ABC of Fifty Thousand Pesos (₱50,000.00) and below.
Also, Section IV(E)(3) of Annex “H” states:
For the following alternative methods, however, the BAC, through its Secretariat, shall post the procurement opportunity [e.g., Request for Quotation (RFQ) or Request for Proposal (RFP)] in the Philippine Government Electronic Procurement System (PhilGEPS) website, the website of the Procuring Entity concerned, if available, and at any conspicuous place reserved for this purpose in the premises of the Procuring Entity for a period of at least three (3) calendar days:
3) Section 53.9 – Small Value Procurement, for projects with ABC above Fifty Thousand Pesos (P50,000.00);
Audit of the DVs and the supporting documents of the payment for the procurements revealed that the BAC, through the Secretariat, did not make the necessary postings, for information purposes, of the notice of awards, the purchase orders, and the RFQs, on the PhilGEPS website, and at any conspicuous places reserved for this purpose in the premises of the procuring entity for a period of at least three (3) calendar days, even though the ABC of these procurements were above Fifty Thousand Pesos (P50,000.00).
Recommendation
We recommended, and Management agreed to require the BAC, through the Secretariat, to make the necessary postings, for information purposes, of the notice of awards, the purchase orders, and the RFQs, on the PhilGEPS website, and at any conspicuous places reserved for this purpose in the premises of the procuring entity for period of at least three (3) calendar days for SVP with ABC above Fifty Thousand Pesos (₱50,000.00) as required by Section 54.3, Rule XV and Section IV(E)(3) Annex “H” of the RIRR of RA 9184.
Eligibility documents such as Mayor’s/ Business Permit and PhilGEPS Registration Number were not attached in the payment for the procurement
Section II, Annex “H”, Appendix A Documentary Requirement Alternative Methods of Procurement states:
The BAC shall indicate in the RFQ or RFP at what stage of the procurement process these requirements must be submitted, i.e., submission of offer/s, evaluation of offer/s, or196 before issuance of Notice of Award.
For Shopping (Sec 52.1 (b)):  Mayor’s/ Business Permit and PhilGEPS Reg. Number
Verification of the DVs and the supporting documents of the payment for the procurements disclosed that the BAC, through the Secretariat, did not support these payments with the Mayor’s/ Business Permit, and PhilGEPS Reg. Number. Without the noted documents, the Audit Team could not validate whether these documents have been submitted during the opening of the RFQs, which could have been the bases for the disqualification of the noncompliant suppliers.
Recommendation
We recommended, and Management agreed to require the BAC, through the Secretariat, to include the supporting documents of the payments for the procurement, through Shopping, the Mayor’s/ Business Permit and PhilGEPS Registration Number as required by Section II, Annex “H” of the RIRR of RA 9184.
Important details of the Purchase Order (PO) such as the Mode of Procurement, the Date of Delivery, and the Terms of Delivery are not indicated therein.
COA Circular No. 96-010 provides the basic data which should invariably appear in the P.O, to wit:
(g) Date of delivery
(o) Terms; i.e., COD, n/30 days, etc.
(p) Mode of procurement;
Examination of the PO of the procurements exposed that the important details therein were not indicated, such as the Mode of Procurement, and the Date and the Terms of Delivery.
The non-indication of the noted details is not in accordance with COA Circular No. 96-010.
Recommendation
We recommended, and Management agreed to require the BAC, through the Secretariat, to indicate in the PO the important details such as the Mode of Procurement, and the Date and the Terms of Delivery to comply with COA Circular No. 96-010.

The General Services Officer shall verify monthly the official travels prepared by the drivers of each motor vehicle and based thereon prepare the Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption showing the type of vehicles, total distance traveled, total fuel used and the normal fuel consumption for each vehicle so that the use of the LGUs motor vehicle can be monitored/controlled and regulated; and
c.) Submit same to the Auditor as required in the regulations to determine the reasonableness of fuel consumed during the period.

Recommendation:

      We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be  required  to  prepare
the bank reconciliation statement for all funds and prepare it regularly  in
accordance with Sec. 74 of PD 1445 and the provisions of  COA  Circular  No.
96-011. Any difference between the cash balance per  bank  versus  the  book
balance must be traced and reconciled to affect the accurate  cash  in  bank
balance.

Recommendation:

      We recommended  to  require  the  Municipal  Assessor  to  submit  the
certified assessment roll to the  Municipal  Treasurer  for  the  latter  to
submit to the Municipal Accountant the certified list of tax payers’  amount
due and collectible for the year to book up  the  estimated  receivables  of
RPT and SET at the beginning of the year.

In view thereof, we recommended that:

      a. The Accounting Department, in collaboration with the Treasury
      Department, perform immediate identification and thorough analysis of
      the recorded trust liabilities and reconcile with the available cash
      balances in the Trust Fund books; and


      b. The Municipal Accountant should also perform periodic
      reconciliation of the trust receipts/transfers coming into the
      possession of the local treasurer with due regard on the proper
      utilization and charges against the trust fund for control and
      monitoring purposes.

Recommendation:

      We recommended that  the municipality  through  the  Municipal  Mayor
should require strict compliance in allocating 1% of  its  Internal  Revenue
Allotment (IRA) as provided for under Section 15 of RA 9344  and  to  ensure
the establishment of LGU’s Local Council for the Protection of  Children  to
spearhead the adoption of a comprehensive plan  on  delinquency  prevention,
and to oversee its proper implementation.

Recommendation:

      We recommended that the  accountable  officers  called  for  attention
herein are required to  submit  explanations  and  justifications  supported
with legal basis  with  regard  to  the  remuneration  given  to  the  LDRRM
Officer.

      Likewise, please submit the following documents in  order  for  us  to
make our additional audit action:

     1. Duly approved appointment of the LDRRM  Officer  confirmed  by  the
        Civil Service Commission;
     2. Budget review by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan for  FY  2017  Annual
        Budget; and
     3. Results of review by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan on  SB  Ordinance
        No. 03 series of 2015.

      It is also  recommended  that  management  should  adjust  immediately
remunerations given to the LDRRM Officer to reflect the  allowable  rate  as
provided  in  NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC  JMC  No.  2014-1.  Meanwhile,  management
should also revisit the guidelines for establishing a  Local  Disaster  Risk
Reduction and Management Office  provided  in  the  same  JMC  and  strictly
adhere with its provisions.

Recommendation:

      In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended that:

   A. Cash advance voucher for the 4th Quarter of  2017  together  with  its
      attachment should be submitted by the Municipal  Accountant/authorized
      representative immediately for post audit. Moreover, there should be a
      timely submission of cash advance voucher and its supporting documents
      to the COA Audit Team Leader which shall not exceed  seven  days  from
      the release of check in accordance with Section 6.1.8.4;


   B. Cash advance voucher and its supporting documents should be  submitted
      by the Municipal Accountant/authorized representative enclosed  in  an
      envelope with a label of confidentiality on  its  face  following  the
      guidelines of Section 6.1.8.4 and item d of 6.3.3;



   C. Lacking supporting documents as noted  for  the  three  (1st  to  3rd)
      quarters must be submitted to the COA ATL concerned. The Head  of  the
      Agency and the Agency Accountant shall  also  ensure  full  compliance
      with  the  necessary  documentary  requirements  as  provided  for  in
      Sections 6.1.7 and 6.1.8.3; and


   D. After liquidation of cash advance, a copy of  the  transmittal  letter
      duly received by  the  ICFAU  should  be  forwarded  to  the  COA  ATL
      concerned notwithstanding to the cash advance to be  made  during  the
      quarter. Responsibilities of the Agency Accountant and the HoA on  the
      granting and liquidation of CF should be religiously  adhered  to  and
      should be in accordance with the  provisions  of  COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND
      Joint Circular No. 2015-01 and other applicable laws.

implementation of PPAs so as not to deprive the  benefits  the  constituents
could have been derived therefrom.
